title: Lambdas & Streams
subtitle: Trade clunky loops for one smooth line that filters, changes, and adds up your data.
goal: Write lambda expressions, use the built-in functional interfaces, and process lists with the Stream API using filter, map, forEach, collect, count, and sum, plus method references.

blocks.heading: A function you can hand over ✨
blocks.html:
Up to now, every piece of code lived inside a method with a name. A lambda is a tiny method with no name that you can store in a variable and pass around like any other value.

The shape is simple: x -> x * 2 reads as “give me an x, and I'll hand back x times 2.” The left side is the input, the arrow -> is the bridge, and the right side is the answer. That's it — a recipe you can carry in your pocket.

blocks.heading: Functional interfaces: the slots lambdas fit into
blocks.html:
A lambda needs a type to live in, and Java gives you ready-made ones in java.util.function. Think of them as labeled slots:

Function<T, R> takes one value, returns another (apply). Predicate<T> asks a yes/no question (test). Consumer<T> takes a value and does something, returning nothing (accept). Supplier<T> takes nothing and produces a value (get). The old friend Runnable takes nothing and returns nothing (run).

blocks.caption: Lambdas plugged into functional interfaces
blocks.output:
double of 5 is 10
is 4 even? true
is 7 even? false
Hello from a lambda!
blocks.explain: doubler is a Function: feed it 5 with apply(5) and it returns 10. isEven is a Predicate: test(4) answers true, test(7) answers false. greet is a Runnable with empty () because it takes no input. Each lambda is just a small recipe stored in a variable.

blocks.html: Empty parentheses mean no input. If a lambda takes nothing you write () -> .... One input can skip the parentheses: x -> x * 2. Two or more need them: (a, b) -> a + b.

blocks.heading: Streams: a pipeline for your list 🌊
blocks.html:
A Stream lets your data flow through a pipeline of steps. You start with list.stream(), then chain steps like filter (keep only what passes a test), map (change each item), and finish with forEach (do something with each one).

Watch the same job — print the even numbers — done the old loop way and the new stream way. Same result, but the stream reads almost like a sentence.

blocks.caption: Before (loop) vs after (stream): print even numbers
blocks.explain: The loop needs a for and an if just to keep the even ones. The stream says it plainly: take the numbers, filter to the evens, forEach print. filter keeps items where the test is true. Both print 2, 4, 6 — the stream just says what you want, not how to loop.

blocks.heading: map, collect, count, and sum
blocks.html:
Streams do more than print. map turns each item into a new one — and instead of writing s -> s.toUpperCase() you can use a method reference: String::toUpperCase, which means “call toUpperCase on each one.”

To gather results into a new list, end with collect(Collectors.toList()). To add numbers up, use mapToInt(...) then sum(). To count what's left, use count().

blocks.caption: Uppercase a list, sum prices, count items
blocks.output:
[ADA, ALAN, GRACE]
Total price: 100
Items over 10: 2
blocks.explain: map(String::toUpperCase) shouts each name, and collect(Collectors.toList()) bundles them into a new list printed as [ADA, ALAN, GRACE]. mapToInt(p -> p).sum() adds 10+25+5+60 = 100. filter(p -> p > 10).count() keeps 25 and 60, so the count is 2. count() returns a long, so howMany is a long.

blocks.html: Streams don't change your list. The original list stays exactly the same — a stream builds a new result instead of editing the old data. That's why collect hands you a brand-new list.

blocks.question: What does the lambda x -> x * 2 do?
options: Takes an input x and returns x times 2
blocks.explain: The left side x is the input, the arrow -> bridges to the result, and x * 2 is what comes back. So feeding it 5 returns 10.

blocks.question: In a stream, what does filter do?
options: Changes every item into something new
Keeps only the items that pass a true/false test
blocks.explain: filter takes a Predicate (a yes/no test) and keeps only the items where it is true. map is the one that changes items; forEach prints them.

blocks.question: What is String::toUpperCase an example of?
options: A method reference, shorthand for s -> s.toUpperCase()
blocks.explain: A method reference is a shorter way to write a lambda that just calls one method. String::toUpperCase means the same as s -> s.toUpperCase().

blocks.title: Uppercase a list with a stream
blocks.instructions: Replace the for-loop with a stream. Use fruits.stream().map(String::toUpperCase).forEach(...) so each fruit prints in CAPITAL letters, one per line.
blocks.passMsg: Clean and smooth — you turned a loop into a one-line pipeline!
blocks.failMsg: Use fruits.stream().map(String::toUpperCase).forEach(f -> System.out.println(f)); to shout each fruit.

blocks.title: Sum the even numbers
blocks.instructions: Build a stream from numbers, filter to keep only the even ones (n % 2 == 0), turn them into ints with mapToInt(n -> n), then sum() them. Store the result in total.
blocks.passMsg: 2 + 4 + 6 + 8 = 20. You chained filter, mapToInt, and sum like a pro!
blocks.failMsg: Chain numbers.stream().filter(n -> n % 2 == 0).mapToInt(n -> n).sum() and store it in total. The answer should be 20.

challenge.title: Shopping cart with streams
challenge.instructions: You have a cart of prices: List.of(15, 8, 40, 3, 22). Using streams, keep only the 'expensive' items where price > 10. Print how many expensive items there are using count(), and the total of those expensive items using mapToInt(price -> price).sum(). Print exactly two lines: 'Expensive items: ' then the count, and 'Total of expensive items: ' then the total.
challenge.solutionOutput:
Expensive items: 3
Total of expensive items: 77
challenge.hint: The prices over 10 are 15, 40, and 22 — that's 3 items, and 15 + 40 + 22 = 77. Run filter(price -> price > 10) twice: once with count(), once with mapToInt(price -> price).sum().
challenge.passMsg: 🏆 Three expensive items totalling 77 — you filtered, counted, and summed with streams. You're writing pro Java now!
